function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
What specific language does the filing use regarding timing of Starship commercial payload delivery, V3 satellite deployment, and V2 Mobile deployment? Compare the wording and assess what it suggests about execution and timing risk.

steps.reasoning_content:
The user is asking about specific language in the SpaceX S-1 filing regarding:
1. Starship commercial payload delivery timing
2. V3 satellite deployment timing
3. V2 Mobile deployment timing

I need to retrieve information from the SpaceX S-1 filing. Let me first parse the filing and then retrieve specific sections about these topics.

Let me start by parsing the S-1 filing.

results.content: SUCCESS: Loaded portable assets for key: spacex_s1

steps.reasoning_content: Now let me retrieve information about Starship commercial payload delivery, V3 satellite deployment, and V2 Mobile deployment from the filing.

arguments.prompt: What specific language does the filing use regarding timing of Starship commercial payload delivery? Find exact quotes about when Starship will deliver commercial payloads, including any forward-looking statements, risk factors, or business descriptions. {{spacex_s1}}

What specific language does the filing use regarding timing of V3 satellite deployment? Find exact quotes about V3 satellites, their deployment schedule, and any forward-looking statements about V3. {{spacex_s1}}

What specific language does the filing use regarding timing of V2 Mobile deployment? Find exact quotes about V2 Mobile, Direct to Cell, deployment schedule, and any forward-looking statements about V2 Mobile. {{spacex_s1}}

results.content:
Based on the provided filing chunks, here are the exact quotes regarding the timing of Starship commercial payload delivery, categorized by their context within the prospectus:

**Forward-Looking Statements**
* "the development and deployment of Starship in accordance with our anticipated schedule (including commencement of payload delivery to orbit in 2026) and launch cadence and our ability to achieve expected performance, reusability, and cost efficiencies;"

**Business Descriptions**
* "We expect Starship to commence payload delivery to orbit in the second half of 2026 following additional flight tests."
* "We expect Starship to commence payload delivery to orbit in the second half of 2026." *(Note: This specific phrasing is repeated multiple times throughout the business descriptions of Starship's capabilities and milestones).*

**Risk Factors**
* "This includes our expectations with respect to completion of flight testing of Starship and commencement of payload delivery to orbit. Our current operational rockets, including Falcon 9 and Falcon Heavy, are not capable of deploying V3 satellites and V2 Mobile satellites."
* "If we are unable to successfully complete the development, testing, and deployment of Starship at scale in accordance with our anticipated schedule, or at all, or if we are unable to achieve sufficient launch cadence, reusability, and capability, our ability to execute our growth strategy (such as the deployment of our next-generation V3 satellites, V2 satellite-to-mobile connectivity, and providing orbital AI compute infrastructure) would be materially and adversely affected."

Based on the provided filing, here are the exact quotes regarding the timing, deployment schedule, and forward-looking statements about V3 satellites, categorized by theme:

### **Deployment Schedule and Timing**
The filing repeatedly pinpoints the second half of 2026 as the expected commencement date for V3 deployment, explicitly tying it to the Starship launch vehicle:

*   "...and in the second half of 2026, we expect to begin deployment of our next-generation V3 satellites, each of which is designed to offer one Tbps of downlink capacity per satellite."
*   "We expect to commence deploying our next-generation V3 satellites, designed to offer one Tbps of downlink capacity per satellite, using Starship in the second half of 2026 and expect that a single Starship launch will be capable of deploying up to 60 V3 satellites to LEO, representing a twenty-fold increase in Starlink downlink capacity deployed relative to a Falcon 9 launch."
*   "“V3 satellites” refers to our next-generation Starlink broadband satellites, which are designed to offer one Tbps of downlink capacity per satellite and which we expect to begin deploying on Starship in the second half of 2026."

### **Forward-Looking Statements and Risk Disclosures**
The preamble contains explicit cautionary language warning that these deployment timelines and capabilities are subject to Starship's development success:

*   "If we are unable to achieve the commercial development, anticipated performance, launch cadence, or cost efficiencies associated with Starship within expected timeframes, our ability to deploy next-generation V3 satellites, V2 Mobile satellites, and orbital AI compute infrastructure at scale... could be materially and adversely affected."
*   "If we are unable to successfully complete the development, testing, and deployment of Starship at scale in accordance with our anticipated schedule, or at all, or if we are unable to achieve sufficient launch cadence, reusability, and capability, our ability to execute our growth strategy (such as the deployment of our next-generation V3 satellites, V2 satellite-to-mobile connectivity, and providing orbital AI compute infrastructure) would be materially and adversely affected."
*   "Our current operational rockets, including Falcon 9 and Falcon Heavy, are not capable of deploying V3 satellites and V2 Mobile satellites."

### **Technical Capabilities and Starship Dependency**
The filing makes forward-looking statements regarding the design capacity of V3 satellites and the infrastructure required to launch them:

*   "We expect Starship will be able to deploy up to 60 V3 satellites per launch, representing a twenty-fold increase in downlink capacity deployed per launch compared to Falcon 9, enabling a more rapid expansion of our Starlink broadband constellation at a significantly lower cost."
*   "Full reusability of Starship’s upper stage is not required to deploy our V3 satellites and V2 Mobile satellites in low-Earth orbit. In-orbit refueling is also not required for any of these LEO programs..."
*   "Starship is a key enabler of our growth objectives, including the deployment of next-generation V3 satellites, direct-to-cell constellations, and orbital AI compute at scale."

### **V3 as a Foundation for Orbital AI Compute**
The filing also looks forward to how the V3 platform will serve as the foundation for future AI infrastructure:

*   "Our V3 satellite platform already incorporates proprietary chips, providing a strong foundation for the ability to operate AI-focused electronics in space, and we expect to begin deploying our orbital AI compute satellites as..."
*   "Specifically, we expect these satellites to leverage our already-designed V3 satellite platform. The core V3 satellite design is complete, and the AI compute satellites are expected to generate substantially more power than V3 satellites."

Based on the provided S-1 filing text, here is the specific language and exact quotes regarding the timing, deployment, and forward-looking statements for V2 Mobile and Direct-to-Cell (satellite-to-mobile) services:

### Timing of V2 Mobile Deployment
The filing explicitly targets 2027 for the deployment of the V2 Mobile satellites and outlines the prerequisites for launching commercial service:
* **"We plan to expand our mobile constellation by deploying our next-generation mobile V2 Mobile satellites in 2027"**
* **"we still require our V2 Mobile satellites to be in orbit and must complete the acquisition of the relevant spectrum from EchoStar before we can commence our planned commercial Gen2 service in the United States."**

### Exact Quotes About V2 Mobile
The filing defines V2 Mobile satellites and outlines their intended capabilities and capacity improvements:
* **“V2 Mobile satellites” refers to our next-generation mobile satellites, which are designed to provide more comprehensive satellite-to-mobile services, including broadband data and IoT connectivity and which we expect**
* **"We plan to expand our mobile constellation by deploying our next-generation mobile V2 Mobile satellites in 2027 which, combined with the EchoStar spectrum acquisition and optimized 5G protocols, are expected to increase capacity by orders of magnitude compared to our first-generation constellation."**
* **"In the United States, we expect to be able to provide 5G-like connectivity to a meaningful portion of existing unmodified devices through our Starlink Mobile Gen2 service utilizing our V2 Mobile satellites, either by operating on spectrum leased to us by MNO partners or by utilizing our own domestic spectrum holdings."**

### Direct to Cell / Satellite-to-Mobile Architecture
The filing refers to "Direct to Cell" primarily as "satellite-to-mobile" or "mobile service," describing the technical architecture as follows:
* **"We are extending the reach of Starlink beyond fixed and mobility terminals through our mobile service, connecting smartphones (with no modifications or incremental hardware) and other terrestrial devices directly to our satellites."**
* **"Our mobile constellation builds on the same LEO architecture as our broadband network, with satellites specifically designed to communicate directly with everyday LTE handsets and IoT devices without requiring specialized or additional hardware."**
* **"The expansion of our global satellite-to-mobile connectivity offerings depends substantially on our ability to enter into and maintain successful partnerships with telecommunications carriers and spectrum licensees globally..."**

### Deployment Schedule & Launch Logistics
The filing details that V2 Mobile deployment relies on Starship, noting that current rockets cannot carry them:
* **"anticipate that Starship will be able to deploy approximately 50 mobile satellites per launch, significantly increasing capacity per launch and accelerating the deployment of our next-generation constellation."**
* **"Full reusability of Starship’s upper stage is not required to deploy our V3 satellites and V2 Mobile satellites in low-Earth orbit."**
* **"Our current operational rockets, including Falcon 9 and Falcon Heavy, are not capable of deploying V3 satellites and V2 Mobile satellites."**

### Forward-Looking Statements About V2 Mobile
The filing includes several forward-looking statements and risk disclosures regarding V2 Mobile, relying on words like "expect" and "plan," and noting dependencies on Starship and regulatory approvals:
* **"If we are unable to achieve the commercial development, anticipated performance, launch cadence, or cost efficiencies associated with Starship within expected timeframes, our ability to deploy next-generation V3 satellites, V2 Mobile satellites, and orbital AI compute infrastructure at scale... could be materially and adversely affected."**
* **"We have an operational coordination agreement with EchoStar, which we expect to continue through 2026 and 2027, under which EchoStar has agreed to protect our lower-priority S-band V2 Mobile constellation."**
* **"We are developing more comprehensive satellite-to-mobile services, which we refer to as our Starlink Mobile Gen2 services, including broadband data and IoT connectivity, which are expected to deliver resilient, infrastructure-independent connectivity worldwide..."**
* **"achieving full 5G NR-NTN compliance and optimal performance would likely require handset manufacturers to implement hardware and software modifications... We do not have direct contractual arrangements with handset manufacturers; instead, we expect MNO partners, as major purchasers of mobile devices, to encourage or drive such adoption."**

steps.message: Let me retrieve additional context on the risk factors and dependencies to better assess execution and timing risk.
steps.reasoning_content: I have a good amount of information now. Let me also look for additional context about the risk factors and any specific language about timing risks. Let me also check for any specific language about the EchoStar spectrum acquisition and its timing.

arguments.prompt: Find exact quotes about risk factors related to Starship development delays, V3 satellite deployment risks, V2 Mobile deployment risks, EchoStar spectrum acquisition timing, and any language about dependencies between these programs. Also find any language about whether these timelines could slip or be delayed. {{spacex_s1}}

Find exact quotes about the EchoStar spectrum acquisition, its expected closing date, regulatory approvals required, and any conditions or contingencies. Also find language about what happens if the EchoStar spectrum acquisition does not close. {{spacex_s1}}

results.content:
Here are the exact quotes from the provided text categorized by the specific risk factors and dependencies you requested:

### **Starship Development Delays**
* "Any failure or delay in the development of Starship at scale or in achieving the required launch cadence, reusability and capabilities thereafter would delay or limit our ability to execute our growth strategy, including the deployment of next-generation satellites, global satellite-to-mobile connectivity, and orbital AI compute, which could materially adversely affect our business, financial condition, results of operations, and future"
* "If we are unable to successfully complete the development, testing, and deployment of Starship at scale in accordance with our anticipated schedule, or at all, or if we are unable to achieve sufficient launch cadence, reusability, and capability, our ability to execute our growth strategy (such as the deployment of our next-generation V3 satellites, V2 satellite-to-mobile connectivity, and providing orbital AI compute infrastructure) would be materially and adversely affected."
* "Delays or challenges in the Starship program have in the past occurred, and may occur in the future due to a variety of factors, including unforeseen technical challenges, supply chain disruptions, manufacturing difficulties, delays in the development, construction or commissioning of launch and fueling infrastructure (such as launch pads, air separation units and other propellant production systems), unavailability of such launch and fueling infrastructure (including launch pads) in sufficient number and in operable condition (including as a result of mishaps), loss or damage to the vehicle or other components, regulatory hurdles, or the need for additional design modifications."
* "If we are required to undertake unanticipated redesigns, conduct additional testing, replace lost vehicles or components, or address operational setbacks, we may experience delays and incur significant additional costs, or be forced to reallocate critical resources from other projects."
* "If our launch pads are not available for an extended period of time for any reason, we may not be able to achieve our development, testing and deployment goals. Such delays could have cascading effects on our ability to achieve the scale we need to timely achieve future objectives."
* "Any significant delay in achieving key development milestones, obtaining the necessary regulatory approvals or increasing and maintaining our launch cadence, reusability, and capability would impede the expansion of our service offerings, defer anticipated revenue streams, and negatively impact our growth trajectory and competitive positioning in rapidly evolving markets."
* "Unexpected design modifications, supply chain disruptions, anomalies, environmental issues, and other unforeseen technical challenges could result in delays or failures to deploy Starship on our anticipated schedule, which would delay or impede our ability to achieve our other business objectives, such as the deployment of our next-generation satellites, the expansion of our satellite -to-mobile connectivity services, and deployment of in-orbit AI compute infrastructure."

### **V3 Satellite Deployment Risks**
* "If we are unable to achieve the commercial development, anticipated performance, launch cadence, or cost efficiencies associated with Starship within expected timeframes, our ability to deploy next-generation V3 satellites, V2 Mobile satellites, and orbital AI compute infrastructure at scale, reduce capital and operating costs (including cost per token), realize projected revenue growth, and retain existing customers from these initiatives could be materially and adversely affected."
* "Our current operational rockets, including Falcon 9 and Falcon Heavy, are not capable of deploying V3 satellites and V2 Mobile satellites."
* "Full reusability of Starship’s upper stage is not required to deploy our V3 satellites and V2 Mobile satellites in low-Earth orbit. In-orbit refueling is also not required for any of these LEO programs and is instead intended for..."

### **V2 Mobile Deployment Risks**
* "Our Gen2 service, which will utilize 2 GHz S-band spectrum and a new satellite constellation, requires a license transfer, a constellation license, and spectrum usage approvals in each country in which we seek to operate."
* "While these authorizations would be sufficient from a United States regulatory perspective, we still require our V2 Mobile satellites to be in orbit and must complete the acquisition of the relevant spectrum from EchoStar before we can commence our planned commercial Gen2 service in the United States."
* "Delays or failures to obtain necessary approvals could materially delay the deployment and commercialization of our Gen2 service."
* "The failure to enter into or successfully maintain such partnerships, or the failure of device manufacturers to adopt the necessary hardware modifications, or the failure to obtain required regulatory approvals, could materially and adversely affect our business, financial condition, results of operations, and future prospects."
* "In the United States, we expect to be able to provide 5G-like connectivity to a meaningful portion of existing unmodified devices through our Starlink Mobile Gen2 service utilizing our V2 Mobile satellites, either by operating on spectrum leased to us by MNO partners or by utilizing our own domestic spectrum holdings. However, achieving full 5G NR-NTN compliance and optimal performance would likely require handset manufacturers to implement hardware and software modifications, primarily to the radio-frequency front end, in future devices."
* "The spectrum frequencies in the FCC licenses to be acquired from EchoStar are standardized for terrestrial 5G mobile broadband (3GPP bands n66 and n70). But the 5G NR-NTN bands for these same frequencies, such as n252 and n256, are not currently supported by RF front-end hardware for the provision of 5G-like service in any commercially available mobile devices."
* "There can be no assurance that these modifications will be adopted on our preferred timeline, or"

### **EchoStar Spectrum Acquisition Timing**
* "In September 2025, we announced a definitive agreement with EchoStar to purchase its AWS-4 and H-block spectrum licenses. The Spectrum Transaction was approved by the FCC on May 12, 2026 and is subject to other closing conditions prior to completion. We expect the Spectrum Transaction to close in November 2027. There can be no assurance that these conditions will be satisfied or waived in a timely manner, or at all."
* "Even if the transaction is completed, there can be no assurance that our purchase of licenses from EchoStar will be sufficient to meet our growing need for spectrum licenses and we may be unable to find other parties to provide us with additional spectrum licenses on terms acceptable to us, or at all."
* "For the United States, we have received the relevant license transfer approval from the FCC, and we expect to receive the remaining necessary regulatory authorizations in the second or third quarter of 2026."

### **Dependencies Between These Programs**
* "Our ability to execute our growth strategy is highly dependent on Starship. If we are unable to achieve the commercial development, anticipated performance, launch cadence, or cost efficiencies associated with Starship within expected timeframes, our ability to deploy next-generation V3 satellites, V2 Mobile satellites, and orbital AI compute infrastructure at scale, reduce capital and operating costs (including cost per token), realize projected revenue growth, and retain existing customers from these initiatives could be materially and adversely affected."
* "Our current operational rockets, including Falcon 9 and Falcon Heavy, are not capable of deploying V3 satellites and V2 Mobile satellites."
* "While these authorizations would be sufficient from a United States regulatory perspective, we still require our V2 Mobile satellites to be in orbit and must complete the acquisition of the relevant spectrum from EchoStar before we can commence our planned commercial Gen2 service in the United States."
* "Starship is a key enabler of our growth objectives, including the deployment of next-generation V3 satellites, direct-to-cell constellations, and orbital AI compute at scale."

### **Language About Timelines Slipping or Being Delayed**
* "There can be no assurance that we will be able to achieve our objectives with respect to Starship within the expected timeframes, if at all, or that delays or setbacks will not materially impact our strategic plans."
* "If we are unable to successfully complete the development, testing, and deployment of Starship at scale in accordance with our anticipated schedule, or at all..."
* "Delays or challenges in the Starship program have in the past occurred, and may occur in the future due to a variety of factors..."
* "If we are required to undertake unanticipated redesigns, conduct additional testing, replace lost vehicles or components, or address operational setbacks, we may experience delays and incur significant additional costs..."
* "If our launch pads are not available for an extended period of time for any reason, we may not be able to achieve our development, testing and deployment goals. Such delays could have cascading effects on our ability to achieve the scale we need to timely achieve future objectives."
* "Any significant delay in achieving key development milestones, obtaining the necessary regulatory approvals or increasing and maintaining our launch cadence, reusability, and capability would impede the expansion of our service offerings, defer anticipated revenue streams..."
* "Unexpected design modifications, supply chain disruptions, anomalies, environmental issues, and other unforeseen technical challenges could result in delays or failures to deploy Starship on our anticipated schedule..."
* "There can be no assurance that these conditions will be satisfied or waived in a timely manner, or at all." *(Regarding the EchoStar spectrum transaction)*
* "Delays or failures to obtain necessary approvals could materially delay the deployment and commercialization of our Gen2 service."
* "Each jurisdiction presents its own regulatory process and timeline, and we cannot predict when or whether approvals will be granted in any given market."
* "There can be no assurance that these modifications will be adopted on our preferred timeline, or" *(Regarding handset hardware modifications for V2 Mobile)*

# EchoStar Spectrum Acquisition: Key Quotes

## Expected Closing Date

> "The Spectrum Acquisition Closing is expected to occur on or about November 30, 2027."

> "We expect the Spectrum Transaction to close in November 2027."

> "We expect the spectrum acquisition to close in November 2027, subject to required regulatory approvals and other closing conditions."

> "The Spectrum Transaction was approved by the FCC on May 12, 2026 and is expected to close on or about November 30, 2027 subject to other closing conditions."

---

## Regulatory Approvals Required

> "The completion of the Spectrum Transactions is subject to the satisfaction or waiver of customary closing conditions, including, among others, receipt of certain consents and approvals from the FCC and the Department of Justice ("DOJ")."

> "The Spectrum Transaction was approved by the FCC on May 12, 2026 and is subject to other closing conditions prior to completion."

> "In the United States, we have received the relevant license transfer approval from the FCC, and we expect to receive the remaining necessary regulatory authorizations in the second or third quarter of 2026."

> "The Foreign Assets will be transferred directly to the Company at the Spectrum Acquisition Closing, to the extent the required regulatory approvals have been obtained by such date; provided, however, that the failure to obtain such approvals will not delay or prevent the Spectrum Acquisition"

---

## Conditions and Contingencies

> "The completion of the Spectrum Transactions is subject to the satisfaction or waiver of customary closing conditions, including, among others, receipt of certain consents and approvals from the FCC and the Department of Justice ("DOJ")."

> "There can be no assurance that these conditions will be satisfied or waived in a timely manner, or at all."

> "The allocation of cash and equity consideration is subject to certain adjustments based on the amount of EchoStar debt satisfied at or prior to closing."

> "Even if the transaction is completed, there can be no assurance that our purchase of licenses from EchoStar will be sufficient to meet our growing need for spectrum licenses and we may be unable to find other parties to provide us with additional spectrum licenses on terms acceptable to us, or at all."

> "We must secure the global right to use the spectrum acquired from EchoStar from a number of international telecommunications regulators in order to make our V2 satellite-to-mobile services usable worldwide, and there can be no assurance that such authorizations will be granted on acceptable terms, or at all."

---

## What Happens If the Acquisition Does Not Close

> "The Spectrum License Purchase Agreement also provides for specified termination rights."

> "As of December 31, 2025, the Spectrum Transfer Closing has not yet occurred, and as a result, the Company is not yet obligated to make any payments under the Credit Agreement with the Trust."

> "Delays or failures to obtain necessary approvals could materially delay the deployment and commercialization of our Gen2 service."

> "We still require our V2 Mobile satellites to be in orbit and must complete the acquisition of the relevant spectrum from EchoStar before we can commence our planned commercial Gen2 service in the United States."

> "The failure to enter into or successfully maintain such partnerships, or the failure of device manufacturers to adopt the necessary hardware modifications, or the failure to obtain required regulatory approvals, could materially and adversely affect our business, financial condition, results of operations, and future prospects."

> "Acquisitions, divestitures, or other strategic transactions we pursue may not achieve the anticipated benefits... including those we expect from the recent acquisition of xAI, the acquisition of spectrum assets and licenses from EchoStar in connection with our Starlink Mobile initiatives... Acquisitions, divestitures, or other strategic transactions may present unforeseen liabilities or disruptions to our operations, which could adversely impact our business, financial condition, results of operations, and future prospects."

steps.message: I now have comprehensive information to answer the question. Let me compile the analysis.
steps.reasoning_content:
I now have a comprehensive understanding of the language used in the filing regarding:
1. Starship commercial payload delivery timing
2. V3 satellite deployment timing
3. V2 Mobile deployment timing

Let me also check for any additional context about the orbital AI compute infrastructure timing, since it's mentioned as a related program.

Actually, I have enough information to provide a comprehensive answer. Let me organize the findings:

**Starship Commercial Payload Delivery:**
- "We expect Starship to commence payload delivery to orbit in the second half of 2026 following additional flight tests."
- Forward-looking statement: "the development and deployment of Starship in accordance with our anticipated schedule (including commencement of payload delivery to orbit in 2026) and launch cadence and our ability to achieve expected performance, reusability, and cost efficiencies;"
- Risk factor: "This includes our expectations with respect to completion of flight testing of Starship and commencement of payload delivery to orbit. Our current operational rockets, including Falcon 9 and Falcon Heavy, are not capable of deploying V3 satellites and V2 Mobile satellites."

**V3 Satellite Deployment:**
- "in the second half of 2026, we expect to begin deployment of our next-generation V3 satellites, each of which is designed to offer one Tbps of downlink capacity per satellite."
- "We expect to commence deploying our next-generation V3 satellites, designed to offer one Tbps of downlink capacity per satellite, using Starship in the second half of 2026 and expect that a single Starship launch will be capable of deploying up to 60 V3 satellites to LEO, representing a twenty-fold increase in Starlink downlink capacity deployed relative to a Falcon 9 launch."
- "V3 satellites" refers to our next-generation Starlink broadband satellites, which are designed to offer one Tbps of downlink capacity per satellite and which we expect to begin deploying on Starship in the second half of 2026."

**V2 Mobile Deployment:**
- "We plan to expand our mobile constellation by deploying our next-generation mobile V2 Mobile satellites in 2027"
- "we still require our V2 Mobile satellites to be in orbit and must complete the acquisition of the relevant spectrum from EchoStar before we can commence our planned commercial Gen2 service in the United States."
- "anticipate that Starship will be able to deploy approximately 50 mobile satellites per launch, significantly increasing capacity per launch and accelerating the deployment of our next-generation constellation."

**EchoStar Spectrum Acquisition:**
- "The Spectrum Acquisition Closing is expected to occur on or about November 30, 2027."
- "The Spectrum Transaction was approved by the FCC on May 12, 2026 and is expected to close on or about November 30, 2027 subject to other closing conditions."
- "There can be no assurance that these conditions will be satisfied or waived in a timely manner, or at all."

Now let me analyze the wording and assess execution and timing risk:

1. **Starship commercial payload delivery (2H 2026):**
   - Uses "expect" - softer than "will"
   - "following additional flight tests" - acknowledges that flight testing is still ongoing
   - Risk factor explicitly states that current rockets cannot deploy V3 or V2 Mobile satellites
   - Acknowledges that delays have occurred in the past and may occur in the future
   - "There can be no assurance that we will be able to achieve our objectives with respect to Starship within the expected timeframes, if at all"

2. **V3 satellite deployment (2H 2026):**
   - Uses "expect to begin deploying" - softer language
   - Tied directly to Starship - "using Starship in the second half of 2026"
   - Risk factor: "If we are unable to achieve the commercial development, anticipated performance, launch cadence, or cost efficiencies associated with Starship within expected timeframes, our ability to deploy next-generation V3 satellites... could be materially and adversely affected."
   - "Our current operational rockets, including Falcon 9 and Falcon Heavy, are not capable of deploying V3 satellites and V2 Mobile satellites."

3. **V2 Mobile deployment (2027):**
   - Uses "plan to expand" - softer language
   - Multiple dependencies: Starship, EchoStar spectrum acquisition (closing Nov 30, 2027), regulatory approvals, MNO partnerships, handset modifications
   - "we still require our V2 Mobile satellites to be in orbit and must complete the acquisition of the relevant spectrum from EchoStar before we can commence our planned commercial Gen2 service in the United States."
   - "There can be no assurance that these conditions will be satisfied or waived in a timely manner, or at all."
   - "There can be no assurance that these modifications will be adopted on our preferred timeline"

Key observations about wording and risk:
- All three programs use hedged language ("expect," "plan," "anticipate") rather than definitive commitments
- All three are dependent on Starship, which itself has acknowledged past delays
- V2 Mobile has the most dependencies (Starship + EchoStar spectrum + regulatory approvals + MNO partnerships + handset modifications)
- The filing explicitly states that current rockets cannot deploy V3 or V2 Mobile satellites, creating a single point of failure
- The filing acknowledges that delays have occurred in the past and may occur in the future
- The filing uses "could be materially and adversely affected" and "would be materially and adversely affected" - the latter is stronger language suggesting greater certainty of adverse impact

Let me also note the timing sequence:
- Starship payload delivery: 2H 2026
- V3 satellite deployment: 2H 2026 (same time as Starship payload delivery)
- V2 Mobile deployment: 2027
- EchoStar spectrum closing: November 30, 2027

This is interesting because V3 deployment is timed to coincide with Starship payload delivery - meaning there's no buffer. If Starship is delayed, V3 is delayed. And V2 Mobile is timed to coincide with the EchoStar spectrum closing - again, no buffer.

Let me also note that the filing uses different verbs:
- Starship: "expect to commence payload delivery"
- V3: "expect to begin deploying" / "expect to commence deploying"
- V2 Mobile: "plan to expand our mobile constellation by deploying"

The use of "plan" for V2 Mobile is slightly softer than "expect" for the others.

Now let me prepare the final answer.

arguments.final_result:
## Specific Filing Language on Starship, V3, and V2 Mobile Timing

### 1. Starship Commercial Payload Delivery — "second half of 2026"

**Business description (repeated multiple times):**
> "We expect Starship to commence payload delivery to orbit in the second half of 2026 following additional flight tests."

**Forward-looking statements section:**
> "the development and deployment of Starship in accordance with our anticipated schedule (including commencement of payload delivery to orbit in 2026) and launch cadence and our ability to achieve expected performance, reusability, and cost efficiencies;"

**Risk factor:**
> "This includes our expectations with respect to completion of flight testing of Starship and commencement of payload delivery to orbit. Our current operational rockets, including Falcon 9 and Falcon Heavy, are not capable of deploying V3 satellites and V2 Mobile satellites."

> "Delays or challenges in the Starship program have in the past occurred, and may occur in the future due to a variety of factors, including unforeseen technical challenges, supply chain disruptions, manufacturing difficulties, delays in the development, construction or commissioning of launch and fueling infrastructure..."

> "There can be no assurance that we will be able to achieve our objectives with respect to Starship within the expected timeframes, if at all, or that delays or setbacks will not materially impact our strategic plans."

---

### 2. V3 Satellite Deployment — "second half of 2026"

**Business description:**
> "...and in the second half of 2026, we expect to begin deployment of our next-generation V3 satellites, each of which is designed to offer one Tbps of downlink capacity per satellite."

> "We expect to commence deploying our next-generation V3 satellites, designed to offer one Tbps of downlink capacity per satellite, using Starship in the second half of 2026 and expect that a single Starship launch will be capable of deploying up to 60 V3 satellites to LEO, representing a twenty-fold increase in Starlink downlink capacity deployed relative to a Falcon 9 launch."

**Glossary definition:**
> "'V3 satellites' refers to our next-generation Starlink broadband satellites, which are designed to offer one Tbps of downlink capacity per satellite and which we expect to begin deploying on Starship in the second half of 2026."

**Risk factor:**
> "If we are unable to achieve the commercial development, anticipated performance, launch cadence, or cost efficiencies associated with Starship within expected timeframes, our ability to deploy next-generation V3 satellites, V2 Mobile satellites, and orbital AI compute infrastructure at scale... could be materially and adversely affected."

> "Our current operational rockets, including Falcon 9 and Falcon Heavy, are not capable of deploying V3 satellites and V2 Mobile satellites."

---

### 3. V2 Mobile Deployment — "2027"

**Business description:**
> "We plan to expand our mobile constellation by deploying our next-generation mobile V2 Mobile satellites in 2027 which, combined with the EchoStar spectrum acquisition and optimized 5G protocols, are expected to increase capacity by orders of magnitude compared to our first-generation constellation."

> "anticipate that Starship will be able to deploy approximately 50 mobile satellites per launch, significantly increasing capacity per launch and accelerating the deployment of our next-generation constellation."

**Risk factor (multi-dependency):**
> "While these authorizations would be sufficient from a United States regulatory perspective, we still require our V2 Mobile satellites to be in orbit and must complete the acquisition of the relevant spectrum from EchoStar before we can commence our planned commercial Gen2 service in the United States."

> "Delays or failures to obtain necessary approvals could materially delay the deployment and commercialization of our Gen2 service."

> "There can be no assurance that these modifications will be adopted on our preferred timeline, or [at all]" (referring to handset hardware modifications for 5G NR-NTN compliance)

**EchoStar spectrum closing (a gating dependency for V2 Mobile commercial service):**
> "The Spectrum Acquisition Closing is expected to occur on or about November 30, 2027."

> "There can be no assurance that these conditions will be satisfied or waived in a timely manner, or at all."

---

## Comparison of Wording

| Program | Stated Timing | Verb Used | Key Dependencies Disclosed |
|---|---|---|---|
| Starship payload delivery | 2H 2026 | "expect to commence" | "following additional flight tests"; past delays acknowledged |
| V3 satellite deployment | 2H 2026 | "expect to begin deploying" / "expect to commence deploying" | Starship only (Falcon 9/Heavy "not capable") |
| V2 Mobile deployment | 2027 | "plan to expand... by deploying" | Starship + EchoStar spectrum closing (Nov 30, 2027) + FCC/DOJ approvals + MNO partnerships + handset hardware modifications |

### Linguistic observations

1. **All three programs use hedged, forward-looking verbs** — "expect," "plan," "anticipate" — rather than definitive commitments ("will"). The V2 Mobile language ("plan to expand... by deploying") is the softest of the three, while Starship and V3 use the slightly firmer "expect to commence/begin."

2. **Starship and V3 share an identical 2H 2026 target with no buffer.** The filing ties V3 deployment directly to Starship's first payload-delivery window — "using Starship in the second half of 2026." There is no disclosed contingency if Starship slips; the filing explicitly states Falcon 9 and Falcon Heavy "are not capable of deploying V3 satellites and V2 Mobile satellites," making Starship a single point of failure for both next-generation constellations.

3. **V2 Mobile carries the most layered dependency stack.** Commercial Gen2 service requires (i) V2 Mobile satellites in orbit (which requires Starship), (ii) completion of the EchoStar spectrum acquisition (closing targeted Nov 30, 2027 — i.e., late in the year, leaving little 2027 runway), (iii) FCC/DOJ and international regulatory approvals, (iv) MNO partnerships, and (v) handset hardware/software modifications for full 5G NR-NTN compliance. The filing notes the relevant 5G NR-NTN bands "are not currently supported by RF front-end hardware for the provision of 5G-like service in any commercially available mobile devices."

4. **The risk-factor language escalates in certainty of adverse impact.** For Starship/V3, the filing uses "could be materially and adversely affected" (conditional). In a separate risk factor it uses the stronger "would be materially and adversely affected" if Starship is not completed "in accordance with our anticipated schedule, or at all." For V2 Mobile, the filing layers multiple "there can be no assurance" clauses across spectrum closing, regulatory approvals, and handset adoption.

5. **The filing explicitly acknowledges historical slippage.** "Delays or challenges in the Starship program have in the past occurred, and may occur in the future" — a candid admission that the 2H 2026 Starship/V3 target and the 2027 V2 Mobile target are not anchored on a program with a spotless on-time record.

---

## Assessment of Execution and Timing Risk

The wording suggests **elevated, cascading timing risk** across all three programs, with risk increasing from Starship → V3 → V2 Mobile:

- **Starship (2H 2026):** The phrase "following additional flight tests" concedes that flight testing is incomplete as of the filing date. The acknowledgment that "delays... have in the past occurred, and may occur in the future" plus the explicit "there can be no assurance... if at all" qualifier indicates management is not committing to the date. The 2H 2026 window is narrow (six months) for a vehicle still in flight test.

- **V3 (2H 2026):** Because V3 deployment is timed to the same 2H 2026 window as Starship's first payload delivery, V3 inherits 100% of Starship's schedule risk with zero disclosed buffer. The filing's statement that current rockets "are not capable of deploying V3 satellites" means there is no fallback path — a Starship slip directly slips V3. The "twenty-fold increase" capacity claim is conditional on Starship achieving 60-satellite-per-launch deployment, which itself depends on Starship reaching operational cadence, not just first payload delivery.

- **V2 Mobile (2027):** This carries the highest aggregate timing risk because it stacks five independent gating conditions, any one of which can delay commercial service: (1) Starship operational, (2) EchoStar spectrum closing by ~Nov 30, 2027, (3) U.S. and international regulatory approvals, (4) MNO partnerships, and (5) handset RF front-end modifications that "are not currently supported... in any commercially available mobile devices." The EchoStar closing date of November 30, 2027 leaves essentially no 2027 commercial-service runway even if all other conditions are met on time, and the filing warns "there can be no assurance that these conditions will be satisfied or waived in a timely manner, or at all."

**Net read:** The filing's language is appropriately cautious — management uses "expect"/"plan" rather than "will," discloses a single-point-of-failure dependency on Starship for both V3 and V2 Mobile, and explicitly admits past Starship delays. The simultaneous 2H 2026 target for both Starship payload delivery and V3 deployment, combined with the multi-dependency 2027 V2 Mobile target gated by a late-November 2027 spectrum closing, suggests the disclosed timelines are aspirational rather than committed, and that slippage of any one program — particularly Starship — would cascade into V3 and V2 Mobile.

---

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm",
      "name": "Space Exploration Technologies Corp. (SpaceX) Form S-1, filed May 20, 2026"
    }
  ]
}

results.content:
## Specific Filing Language on Starship, V3, and V2 Mobile Timing

### 1. Starship Commercial Payload Delivery — "second half of 2026"

**Business description (repeated multiple times):**
> "We expect Starship to commence payload delivery to orbit in the second half of 2026 following additional flight tests."

**Forward-looking statements section:**
> "the development and deployment of Starship in accordance with our anticipated schedule (including commencement of payload delivery to orbit in 2026) and launch cadence and our ability to achieve expected performance, reusability, and cost efficiencies;"

**Risk factor:**
> "This includes our expectations with respect to completion of flight testing of Starship and commencement of payload delivery to orbit. Our current operational rockets, including Falcon 9 and Falcon Heavy, are not capable of deploying V3 satellites and V2 Mobile satellites."

> "Delays or challenges in the Starship program have in the past occurred, and may occur in the future due to a variety of factors, including unforeseen technical challenges, supply chain disruptions, manufacturing difficulties, delays in the development, construction or commissioning of launch and fueling infrastructure..."

> "There can be no assurance that we will be able to achieve our objectives with respect to Starship within the expected timeframes, if at all, or that delays or setbacks will not materially impact our strategic plans."

---

### 2. V3 Satellite Deployment — "second half of 2026"

**Business description:**
> "...and in the second half of 2026, we expect to begin deployment of our next-generation V3 satellites, each of which is designed to offer one Tbps of downlink capacity per satellite."

> "We expect to commence deploying our next-generation V3 satellites, designed to offer one Tbps of downlink capacity per satellite, using Starship in the second half of 2026 and expect that a single Starship launch will be capable of deploying up to 60 V3 satellites to LEO, representing a twenty-fold increase in Starlink downlink capacity deployed relative to a Falcon 9 launch."

**Glossary definition:**
> "'V3 satellites' refers to our next-generation Starlink broadband satellites, which are designed to offer one Tbps of downlink capacity per satellite and which we expect to begin deploying on Starship in the second half of 2026."

**Risk factor:**
> "If we are unable to achieve the commercial development, anticipated performance, launch cadence, or cost efficiencies associated with Starship within expected timeframes, our ability to deploy next-generation V3 satellites, V2 Mobile satellites, and orbital AI compute infrastructure at scale... could be materially and adversely affected."

> "Our current operational rockets, including Falcon 9 and Falcon Heavy, are not capable of deploying V3 satellites and V2 Mobile satellites."

---

### 3. V2 Mobile Deployment — "2027"

**Business description:**
> "We plan to expand our mobile constellation by deploying our next-generation mobile V2 Mobile satellites in 2027 which, combined with the EchoStar spectrum acquisition and optimized 5G protocols, are expected to increase capacity by orders of magnitude compared to our first-generation constellation."

> "anticipate that Starship will be able to deploy approximately 50 mobile satellites per launch, significantly increasing capacity per launch and accelerating the deployment of our next-generation constellation."

**Risk factor (multi-dependency):**
> "While these authorizations would be sufficient from a United States regulatory perspective, we still require our V2 Mobile satellites to be in orbit and must complete the acquisition of the relevant spectrum from EchoStar before we can commence our planned commercial Gen2 service in the United States."

> "Delays or failures to obtain necessary approvals could materially delay the deployment and commercialization of our Gen2 service."

> "There can be no assurance that these modifications will be adopted on our preferred timeline, or [at all]" (referring to handset hardware modifications for 5G NR-NTN compliance)

**EchoStar spectrum closing (a gating dependency for V2 Mobile commercial service):**
> "The Spectrum Acquisition Closing is expected to occur on or about November 30, 2027."

> "There can be no assurance that these conditions will be satisfied or waived in a timely manner, or at all."

---

## Comparison of Wording

| Program | Stated Timing | Verb Used | Key Dependencies Disclosed |
|---|---|---|---|
| Starship payload delivery | 2H 2026 | "expect to commence" | "following additional flight tests"; past delays acknowledged |
| V3 satellite deployment | 2H 2026 | "expect to begin deploying" / "expect to commence deploying" | Starship only (Falcon 9/Heavy "not capable") |
| V2 Mobile deployment | 2027 | "plan to expand... by deploying" | Starship + EchoStar spectrum closing (Nov 30, 2027) + FCC/DOJ approvals + MNO partnerships + handset hardware modifications |

### Linguistic observations

1. **All three programs use hedged, forward-looking verbs** — "expect," "plan," "anticipate" — rather than definitive commitments ("will"). The V2 Mobile language ("plan to expand... by deploying") is the softest of the three, while Starship and V3 use the slightly firmer "expect to commence/begin."

2. **Starship and V3 share an identical 2H 2026 target with no buffer.** The filing ties V3 deployment directly to Starship's first payload-delivery window — "using Starship in the second half of 2026." There is no disclosed contingency if Starship slips; the filing explicitly states Falcon 9 and Falcon Heavy "are not capable of deploying V3 satellites and V2 Mobile satellites," making Starship a single point of failure for both next-generation constellations.

3. **V2 Mobile carries the most layered dependency stack.** Commercial Gen2 service requires (i) V2 Mobile satellites in orbit (which requires Starship), (ii) completion of the EchoStar spectrum acquisition (closing targeted Nov 30, 2027 — i.e., late in the year, leaving little 2027 runway), (iii) FCC/DOJ and international regulatory approvals, (iv) MNO partnerships, and (v) handset hardware/software modifications for full 5G NR-NTN compliance. The filing notes the relevant 5G NR-NTN bands "are not currently supported by RF front-end hardware for the provision of 5G-like service in any commercially available mobile devices."

4. **The risk-factor language escalates in certainty of adverse impact.** For Starship/V3, the filing uses "could be materially and adversely affected" (conditional). In a separate risk factor it uses the stronger "would be materially and adversely affected" if Starship is not completed "in accordance with our anticipated schedule, or at all." For V2 Mobile, the filing layers multiple "there can be no assurance" clauses across spectrum closing, regulatory approvals, and handset adoption.

5. **The filing explicitly acknowledges historical slippage.** "Delays or challenges in the Starship program have in the past occurred, and may occur in the future" — a candid admission that the 2H 2026 Starship/V3 target and the 2027 V2 Mobile target are not anchored on a program with a spotless on-time record.

---

## Assessment of Execution and Timing Risk

The wording suggests **elevated, cascading timing risk** across all three programs, with risk increasing from Starship → V3 → V2 Mobile:

- **Starship (2H 2026):** The phrase "following additional flight tests" concedes that flight testing is incomplete as of the filing date. The acknowledgment that "delays... have in the past occurred, and may occur in the future" plus the explicit "there can be no assurance... if at all" qualifier indicates management is not committing to the date. The 2H 2026 window is narrow (six months) for a vehicle still in flight test.

- **V3 (2H 2026):** Because V3 deployment is timed to the same 2H 2026 window as Starship's first payload delivery, V3 inherits 100% of Starship's schedule risk with zero disclosed buffer. The filing's statement that current rockets "are not capable of deploying V3 satellites" means there is no fallback path — a Starship slip directly slips V3. The "twenty-fold increase" capacity claim is conditional on Starship achieving 60-satellite-per-launch deployment, which itself depends on Starship reaching operational cadence, not just first payload delivery.

- **V2 Mobile (2027):** This carries the highest aggregate timing risk because it stacks five independent gating conditions, any one of which can delay commercial service: (1) Starship operational, (2) EchoStar spectrum closing by ~Nov 30, 2027, (3) U.S. and international regulatory approvals, (4) MNO partnerships, and (5) handset RF front-end modifications that "are not currently supported... in any commercially available mobile devices." The EchoStar closing date of November 30, 2027 leaves essentially no 2027 commercial-service runway even if all other conditions are met on time, and the filing warns "there can be no assurance that these conditions will be satisfied or waived in a timely manner, or at all."

**Net read:** The filing's language is appropriately cautious — management uses "expect"/"plan" rather than "will," discloses a single-point-of-failure dependency on Starship for both V3 and V2 Mobile, and explicitly admits past Starship delays. The simultaneous 2H 2026 target for both Starship payload delivery and V3 deployment, combined with the multi-dependency 2027 V2 Mobile target gated by a late-November 2027 spectrum closing, suggests the disclosed timelines are aspirational rather than committed, and that slippage of any one program — particularly Starship — would cascade into V3 and V2 Mobile.

---
